The US jobs report is out:
June payrolls came in at 57,000, falling short of the consensus estimate, while the unemployment rate edged down to 4.2% (versus the expected 4.3%).
Combining these figures with other data in the report—including a dip in labor force participation to 61.5% and 3.5% earnings growth—suggests that the supply-side of the labor force was the primary driver for the miss in job creation.
As
To implications for Fed policy, this should dampen market expectations for a rate hike this year—a scenario I have argued was a misreading of the Fed’s likely stance.
#economy #jobs #unemployment #employment #markets #federalreserve

tweets.text:
BREAKING: The Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR) fell -5.5 million barrels last week, to 326 million barrels, the lowest since May 1983.

This marks the 13th consecutive weekly decline, the longest streak since the 2021-2023 period, per Zerohedge.

Over this time period, US oil reserves in the SPR have fallen -89 million barrels.

This represents 51.7% of the planned 172 million barrels to be released under a relief program coordinated by the IEA to lower energy costs.

By comparison, the US added +69 million barrels of oil to the SPR between July 2023 and March 2026.

US oil reserves are being depleted at a historic pace.

Retail dip-buying is reaching historic extremes:

Average daily equity purchases by retail investors during S&P 500 down days are running at nearly 3.5 times the daily average seen since 2020, the highest on record.

This is +56% higher than during the meme stock mania in 2021.

Year-to-date, retail purchases on down days are 2.3 times larger than on up days.

Even during S&P 500 up days, retail investors purchased nearly 1.5 times the daily average seen since 2020, so far this year, and double the figure posted in 2025.

Individual investors have consistently purchased more equities on down days than on up days in each of the last 7 years.

Retail investors are eager to buy dips.

History points to a strong July for US stocks:

Since 2005, the S&P 500 has recorded an average gain of +2.5% in July, more than 4 times the average increase seen in the other 11 months of the year.

The index has not declined in July for 11 consecutive years, the longest positive streak of any month over this period.

Meanwhile, the S&P 500 has posted 24 all-time highs so far in 2026.

This ranks among the top 20 strongest starts to a year for the index since World War II..

Historically, when the index has seen such a streak, it has rallied another +6% over the following 6 months, on average.

Seasonal patterns suggest continued bullish momentum this month.

tweets.text:
US-hiring plans remain historically depressed:

US-based employers announced plans to add 10,933 jobs in June, marking a -44% decline from 19,536 in May.

Year-to-date, US hiring plans rose +10% YoY, to 91,405 workers.

This comes as technology sector hiring plans surged +167% YoY, to 14,761 workers.

However, excluding technology, hiring plans are down -1% YoY, to 76,644 workers.

The decline has been driven by entertainment and leisure hiring plans, which are down -57% YoY to 12,096 workers so far in 2026.

This also follows 5 consecutive years of declining hiring intentions, which fell -84% since 2020, to 508,000 for the full year 2025.

Hiring continues to weaken beneath the surface.

thread_tweets.text:
Not every stablecoin is built the same. DAI cannot be frozen or upgraded. USDS can. They share the same backing. That is by design.

While they share the same backing and overlap on many qualities, their design priorities diverge. DAI leans on sovereignty and transparency. USDS optimizes for accessibility and yield. @rhadiARK unpacks the tradeoffs across five criteria: transparency, durability, sovereignty, accessibility, and incentives in part 3 of ARK’s Guide To Stablecoins.

Read the full research blog.
https://t.co/VRbq6HquBP

Let’s do some napkin math together 

To raise $65m with a 4-yr locked OTC deal, what price on the tokens would a rational buyer accept? Well below $10, maybe $8?

That’d mean selling 8.1m tokens. We would never offer such a thing.

Instead we raised $65m by only selling 1.5m tokens locked for four years, and agreed to sell 5m more for an additional $66m, also locked for four years.

If exercised, it means we got $131m for 6.5m tokens locked, rather than your suggestion of $65m for ~8m tokens

The “first class citizen” investors are taking substantial risk as their equity and tokens are all illiquid/locked unlike every token holder today. The only scenario under which they’d do so is with long term belief in Venice as a business, taking a bet that both equity and tokens will appreciate materially, together, as they have done to date.

tweets.text:
We are at levels where if you can look ahead 12-18 months then you will do very well. 

You will always convince yourself that lower is going to happen when price actually moves towards your target. 

If you see that an alt is 85-95% down, has a working product with some traction, the fundamentals have improved, and token holders are the main beneficiaries, then you go for it imo.

You should always be willing to stomach some downside if you want outsized performance. You can manage this process by buying later on confirmation, but you actually give up a lot of outperformance for some peace of mind.

Personally, I think if you have done your research to develop conviction then you should be able to ride some end of the bear market type downside, but each to their own. 

Do whatever allows you to see through the process of holding for a long period of time to capture big upside. 

Crypto is not dead.

tweets.text:
I took a photo in a cathedral in Valletta.

I didn’t know why it stopped me.

I’m starting to.

I was standing in St John’s, in front of a painting I couldn’t walk away from.

I forgot about it until today when I saw it in my camera roll and noticed what it said: “CLAUDIT DUM RESERAT” so…

I asked GPT what these latin words meant that were older than anyone alive.

“IT CLOSES WHILE IT OPENS” 

I didn’t feel the weight of that until I thought about where I am right now.

For the first time, I’m taking profit. Real profit. The kind that moves a life.

I’m buying a home. My first.

You have to understand what I’m walking away from.

In this game you are one smart contract from losing all of it.

One trade from zero.
One exploit from watching it vanish while you sleep.
Nothing you hold is ever really yours. It’s on loan from the next block.

And to keep a roof over my head I’ve been paying rent into a hole. Money gone, every month, for the privilege of standing still.

There’s gold in the game. The edge. The speed. The proof you can make something from nothing. That is going to continue but from a new perspective.

There’s rot in it too. It never lets you stop. There is always more, and more is always one click from gone. 

Owning most of a mortgage doesn’t make me rich.

It makes me free.

Free from the rat race. Free from the need to constantly do more.

Then there’s the image itself.

A skull. A crown beside it.

That’s the whole sermon, in two objects.

The crown is power. Ego. Status. 

Everything we chase.

The skull is the receipt.

No king, no warrior, no artist, no rich man, no poor man walks away from the same final count.

“IT CLOSES WHILE IT OPENS” combined with that strong imagery means:

Death closes the door of life. And in closing it, it unlocks the truth of what the life was.

Every possession vanquished.
The number stops mattering.
The theatre of status goes dark.

And what’s left is the only real question. 

What did you do with the time?

If life didn’t end, nothing would matter.

You could delay who you are forever.

The closing is what makes the living serious.

Mortality is what hands you courage, and priority, and the nerve to choose.

It’s only because it ends, you are forced to decide what deserves your life.

In buying a house, I’m unlocking more time.

In unlocking time I’m able to start a family.

In unlocking time I get to live a life with real meaning.

I used to tell myself more was the point.

Now I’m taking chips off the table.

“It closes while it opens”

Go live.

There's $1.2T of Bitcoin sitting in wallets right now.

Less than 1% of it earns anything.

Compare that with 28% of $ETH and 65% of $SOL already earning yield through staking.

Most BTC just sits in wallets doing nothing, which is kinda wild when you think about the size of it.

Been looking at how Stacks approaches this with Dual Stacking.

You can hold sBTC and earn around 0.5% APY, or pair it with $STX through Dual Stacking and boost that up to around 5% APY in sBTC.

The key difference is that the rewards stay BTC-denominated. No points program or random token airdrops. You're just earning more of the thing you already hold.

One thing to keep in mind is that the higher yield comes with STX exposure, so your actual outcome can change if STX moves against you.

There are a few other small signals stacking up too.
STX also showed up in Coinbase's COIN50 index recently. 

It's a small thing, but it's another sign that Bitcoin DeFi is slowly becoming its own category instead of an afterthought.

None of these is a massive catalyst on its own.

The interesting part is the direction...the largest pool of idle capital in crypto is slowly getting reasons to do something.

Glad to partner with @Stacks to highlight how Bitcoin yield is starting to move beyond just holding.

tweets.text:
$TSLA deliveries are the first sign we're exiting the EV winter that started in March of 2024.

June Deliveries were up 25% y/y (vs. the Street up 6% and the high-end estimate of 21%). This was the strongest delivery number since Sep'23, which was up 27% y/y. Additionally, June Deliveries would have been up 27% if not for the S/X sunset.

My guess is higher gas prices worldwide likely impacted deliveries by 5%. In other words, the strength in deliveries tells us something bigger is going on. That is Tesla's fundamentally the most appealing EV on the market (in the U.S.) and more broadly, consumers are returning to EVs.

Deliveries still matter because more vehicles means more shots on goal for autonomy.

tweets.text:
We actually found $INSM by accident while researching $ABVX when it was trading around $95(now at $138).

As part of our due diligence, we spoke with our biotech expert and started digging into who the smartest shareholders were. One name immediately stood out: Darwin Global. They had roughly 9% of their portfolio invested in $ABVX.

When I asked our biotech hedge fund expert about them, his response was simple: “That’s probably the best biotech hedge fund of the last five years.” If I remember correctly, he said they compounded their portfolio by roughly 10x through a handful of highly concentrated bets.

Today, around 68% of Darwin Global’s reported equity portfolio is invested in $INSM, with $ABVX representing another major position.

That doesn’t prove the investment thesis is right, of course. But when a specialist biotech fund with that kind of track record is willing to make such an outsized bet, my assumption is that they’ve done an extraordinary amount of work. That was one of the reasons we decided to dig much deeper into the company ourselves.

The NASDAQ is 97.5% correlated to total global liquidity.

It has almost nothing to do with earnings or how good the companies are, and everything to do with how much money the world's central banks are printing.

The small slice that liquidity doesn't explain is the NASDAQ's own adoption curve sitting on top.

This is why everyone keeps saying equities are expensive. They're using a measure that stopped working the moment we started debasing currency. A high valuation doesn't really tell you a company is doing well anymore, it tells you how much money has been printed.

This is the heart of my Everything Code framework. Once you understand that liquidity is the key driver of all asset prices, the market stops behaving like a mystery and starts running like clockwork.

By checks, I mean people who check on other people to make sure they're performing well, and by balances, I mean balances of power. Even the most benevolent leaders are prone to becoming more autocratic, if for no other reason than because managing a lot of people and having limited time to do it requires them to make numerous difficult choices quickly, and they sometimes lose patience with arguments and issue commands instead. And most leaders are not so benevolent that they can be trusted to put the organization's interests ahead of their own.
#principleoftheday

For our 10th season, we are celebrating the 250th year of the USA by concentrating the entire Meet the Drapers season on the US. We've already taken Meet the Drapers through Tuscaloosa, Alabama, Detroit, Michigan, New York City, New York, Nashville, Tennessee, Austin, Texas, Salt Lake City, Utah, and Honolulu, Hawaii this season.

Each stop is better than the one before. Word has gotten out in the US about the show and we are getting more and better contestant entrepreneurs for the show.

The next generation of world-changing companies is being discovered, created and vetted through new models like Meet The Drapers.

In this Meet The Drapers episode, I got to share the stage with my son Adam Draper (Boost VC), former U.S. Treasurer Rosie Rios, and James Walker and the energy was incredible.

tweets.text:
I'm expecting a trend change in crypto today if payrolls data (NFP) comes in soft.

If not today, then the trend change will have to wait for the market to price in higher odds of no hike at the July FOMC (Jul/29). The next catalysts for that repricing are the FOMC minutes (Jul/8) and CPI (Jul/14). If CPI comes in hot, the repricing waits until the July FOMC itself.

NFP, CPI, the minutes, and FOMC speeches are more important than ever given Warsh's no forward guidance policy.

I expect no hike on Jul/29, given the Middle East cease fire and inflation having peaked, but the market is not so sure and needs data to hold its hand until then.

A hike would be extremely bearish. Without forward guidance, the market would have a hard time distinguishing between an "insurance hike" and the start of a hiking cycle.

The main drag on crypto in the last six weeks has been BTC and Saylor, whose unfortunate actions (using cash reserves to repurchase 2029 converts + making a test bitcoin sale) drove price from 82K to 58K. That risk is now mostly gone, at least for the foreseeable future.

Meanwhile, the main drags on equities and risk assets in general in the last two weeks have been a) the US-Iran cease fire exhausting most of the de-escalation upside, b) a hawkish June FOMC, and c) reignited AI bubble fears (misplaced IMO).

​
Which wins? The one whose asymmetric bets survive the bear.

tweets.text:
One very simple reason the Fed and Treasury can’t afford to raise interest rates and why the inflationistas are likely to be proven wrong.  

From Torsten Slok:

“A wave of corporate bonds issued during the low-rate era of 2020 and 2021 is now coming due, forcing companies to refinance cheap debt at today's higher rates, with high-yield borrowers feeling the squeeze first given their shorter 5-to-8-year maturities, while investment-grade issuers sit in a stronger position today, having locked in low rates with 10-year-plus tenors that push their refinancing needs comfortably further out.”

Pair this with the refinancing needs of over $1 trillion at the Treasury and I highly doubt will see a hawkish Fed’s

Credit spreads rising should keep expectations for any rate rising at bay. 

@infraa_
